reasoning: The Lean statement captures all the content of the informal statement: `G` is an arbitrary group, `a, b` are arbitrary elements, the hypothesis `a * b = b * a` matches `ab = ba`, and the conclusion `∀ x y : closure {x | x = a ∨ x = b}, x * y = y * x` says every pair of elements of the subgroup generated by {a, b} commute — which is exactly the definition of H being abelian (since the subgroup inherits its multiplication from G). `Subgroup.closure` on `{x | x = a ∨ x = b}` = `{a, b}` is precisely "the subgroup generated by a and b." The shadowing of `x` inside the set-builder versus the outer `∀ x y` is cosmetic (the inner `x` is locally bound to the setOf) and does not change the meaning. This is a faithful formalization.

The Lean statement correctly encodes "contains exactly one element of order 2" by combining existence (`orderOf x = 2`) with uniqueness (`∀ y, orderOf y = 2 → y = x`). The conclusion `x ∈ center G` matches Mathlib's `Subgroup.center`, whose membership is `∀ g, g * x = x * g` — the standard group-theoretic center. The implicit universal quantifications over the group G and the element x correctly capture the informal "a group... that element". No extra hypotheses are added and no hypotheses are dropped, so this is a logically equivalent formalization.

The informal statement says "a group of even order contains an element of order 2". The Lean statement encodes this as: for any finite group G (with `[Fintype G]`) such that `Even (card G)`, there exists x with `orderOf x = 2`. The Fintype hypothesis is implicit in the informal phrase "even order" — speaking of a group's order presupposes finiteness. `Even (card G)` correctly captures "even order", and `∃ x : G, orderOf x = 2` correctly captures "contains an element of order 2". The quantifier structure (universal over G, existential over x) matches. This is the standard Cauchy-for-p=2 formulation and is logically equivalent to the informal claim.

The Lean statement correctly encodes "V is a K-vector space" via `Field K`, `AddCommGroup V`, `Module K V`. The spanning-by-countably-infinite-set hypothesis is captured precisely by `Set.Countable S ∧ S.Infinite ∧ span K S = ⊤` (Mathlib's `Set.Countable` means at-most-countable, so combined with `S.Infinite` this gives countably infinite). The conclusion `Countable ι` (from Mathlib's `Data.Countable.Defs`) means "at most countable", exactly matching "finite or countably infinite". The encoding of "every linearly independent subset" via universally-quantified families `R : ι → V` with `LinearIndependent K R` is equivalent: any linearly independent set T gives rise to the family indexed by T (subtype), and conversely, since `Field K` implies `Nontrivial K`, linear independence of R forces R injective, so ι is in bijection with its linearly-independent image in V. No hypotheses are silently added or dropped.

The informal statement asserts: if G/Z(G) is cyclic then G is abelian (equivalently, G = Z). The Lean theorem takes `hG : IsCyclic (G ⧸ center G)` and concludes `center G = ⊤`. In Mathlib, `center G = ⊤` (as a Subgroup) is exactly the condition that every element of G commutes with every other, i.e., G is abelian / G = Z (confirmed by `CommGroup.center_eq_top` and `Group.commGroupOfCenterEqTop` in GroupTheory/Subgroup/Center.lean, which show the equivalence). The hypothesis `IsCyclic (G ⧸ center G)` directly matches "G/Z is cyclic". No hypotheses are added or dropped, and the universal quantification over groups G is preserved. The two statements are logically equivalent.

The Lean signature universally quantifies (via implicit arguments) over all finite groups G and natural number primes p, q with |G| = p²q, and asserts ¬IsSimpleGroup G (encoded as IsSimpleGroup G → False). Every element of the informal statement is captured: `Fintype G` + `card G = p^2 * q` states "G has order p²q"; `Prime p` and `Prime q` on ℕ coincide with `Nat.Prime` via `Nat.prime_iff`; `IsSimpleGroup` is Mathlib's standard notion of a simple group (nontrivial, with only ⊥ and ⊤ as normal subgroups). The informal statement does not require p ≠ q, and neither does the Lean statement — and the theorem remains true in the p = q case (|G| = p³ is a nontrivial p-group with nontrivial center, hence not simple). No hypotheses are added or dropped, no quantifiers are flipped, no types are changed.

The informal statement asserts that {a, b} and {bab², bab³} generate the same subgroup of G. The Lean statement uses `closure` (which resolves to `Subgroup.closure` due to `open Subgroup`) to assert the equality `Subgroup.closure {a, b} = Subgroup.closure {b*a*b^2, b*a*b^3}`. This precisely expresses "the subgroup generated by {a,b} equals the subgroup generated by {bab², bab³}." The group G is a general/arbitrary group (matching the universal "a group"), the elements a, b : G are arbitrary, and the types of the sets are correct (Set G). No extra hypotheses are added and none are dropped. This is a direct, equivalent formalization.

The informal statement claims that in the ring ℤ[x], the intersection of the ideals (2) and (x) equals the ideal (2x). The Lean theorem uses `Polynomial ℤ` for ℤ[x], `Ideal.span {2}` / `Ideal.span {X}` for the ideals (2) and (x), `Ideal.span {2 * X}` for (2x), and `⊓` for the lattice meet of ideals. Since `open Ideal` is active and `span` takes a single argument, `span` resolves to `Ideal.span` rather than `Submodule.span` (which requires the scalar ring explicitly). The meet `⊓` on ideals coincides with set-theoretic intersection (verified via `Submodule.coe_inf`). Thus the Lean statement asserts exactly the claimed equality of ideals with no added or dropped hypotheses.

The Lean statement precisely encodes the informal claim. `x : ↑(I ⊓ J)` is an element of I ∩ J (since `Ideal.mem_inf` gives `x ∈ I ⊓ J ↔ x ∈ I ∧ x ∈ J`), and `Ideal.Quotient.mk (I*J) x` is the residue of x in R/(IJ), using Mathlib's canonical ideal product and quotient map. `IsNilpotent` is the standard "∃n, x^n = 0" notion. The CommRing assumption is the standard implicit premise needed for "R/IJ" to unambiguously form a ring quotient in the conventional setting of the informal statement (since `Ideal R` in Mathlib denotes left ideals, and the ring product of ideals plus ring quotient structure most naturally lives in the commutative setting); making it explicit is a faithful reading, not a strengthening. The mathematical content matches: x ∈ I ∩ J implies x² = x·x ∈ I·J, so [x]² = 0 in R/IJ, making [x] nilpotent.

The Lean statement captures exactly the informal claim. The hypothesis `hM` directly encodes "every element not in M is a unit", and the conclusion `IsMaximal M ∧ ∀ N, IsMaximal N → N = M` correctly expresses "M is a maximal ideal and is the unique maximal ideal". The added hypothesis `hProper : ∃ x, x ∉ M` (equivalent to M being a proper ideal) surfaces a necessary implicit premise: if M = R, the hypothesis `hM` is vacuously satisfied but the conclusion "M is maximal" fails (Mathlib's `IsMaximal` requires `IsCoatom`, i.e., M ≠ ⊤), and in the zero ring no maximal ideals exist at all. Since the informal phrasing "every element of R not in M is a unit" naturally presupposes non-M is nonempty and the conclusion requires M to be proper, this is surfacing an implicit premise, not narrowing scope. Mathlib's `Ideal R` (left ideals) with `[Ring R]` is a standard reading of "ideal of a ring", and the result holds at this level of generality.

The Lean statement `Irreducible (12 + 6 * X + X ^ 3 : Polynomial ℚ)` encodes exactly the informal claim. The polynomial `12 + 6*X + X^3` is the same as $x^3 + 6x + 12$ (just written with ascending coefficients), `Polynomial ℚ` is $\mathbb{Q}[x]$, and Mathlib's `Irreducible` predicate captures the standard ring-theoretic notion of irreducibility (not a unit, and any factorization has a unit factor), which is precisely what "irreducible in ℚ" means for a polynomial. No hypotheses are added or dropped, and no quantifier structure is changed.

The informal claim is "X^2+1 is irreducible in F_7". The Lean encoding quantifies over any field F with a Fintype instance and card F = 7, and asserts Irreducible (X^2+1) in Polynomial F. By the classification of finite fields, any such F is (uniquely up to isomorphism) the field ZMod 7 = F_7, and ring isomorphism preserves irreducibility of polynomials. Hence "for all fields F with |F|=7, X^2+1 is irreducible in F[X]" is logically equivalent to "X^2+1 is irreducible in F_7". The conclusion uses Polynomial.Irreducible correctly on X^2+1. No hypotheses are added or dropped beyond what is needed to specify "the" field of order 7.

The Lean statement captures the informal claim: for `p` a prime natural number (the standard reading of "prime integer" in textbooks, encoded via `Prime p` on `ℕ`), the polynomial `X^n - p` in `ℚ[X]` is irreducible. The coercion `(p : Polynomial ℚ)` gives the constant polynomial `p`, matching `x^n - p`. The only non-literal addition is `hn : n > 0`, which is an implicit premise needed for truth: at `n = 0`, `X^0 - p = 1 - p` is a nonzero rational constant (since `p ≥ 2`), hence a unit in `ℚ[X]`, hence NOT irreducible in Mathlib's convention. Any mathematician reading "the polynomial $x^n - p$" would implicitly assume $n \geq 1$ (otherwise there is no polynomial equation to speak of in the intended sense). Per the guidance, surfacing such an implicit premise needed for the statement to be true is faithful, not weaker. Thus the formalization is strictly faithful.

This matches the system prompt's worked example exactly. The informal statement says a prime of the form 2^r + 1 must have the form 2^{2^k} + 1. Literally, without r ≥ 1, the claim fails at r = 0: p = 2 is prime but is not 2^{2^k} + 1 for any k (as 2^{2^k} + 1 ≥ 3). The Lean formalization adds `1 ≤ r` to make this implicit premise explicit — a standard and correct reading of the intended mathematical content. The rest of the encoding is faithful: p is prime (ℕ vs ℤ is inconsequential since 2^r + 1 ≥ 3 > 0), the hypothesis is an existence of r with p = 2^r + 1, and the conclusion is an existence of k with p = 2^(2^k) + 1, preserving the quantifier structure.

The informal statement asserts that -(-v) = v for every v in a vector space V. The Lean formalization sets up F as a Field, V as an AddCommGroup with a Module F V structure (the standard Mathlib encoding of "vector space V over field F"), and asserts -(-v) = v for an implicit v : V (giving universal quantification over V). The hypotheses, conclusion, quantifier structure, and types all match the informal statement exactly. While the property -(-v) = v actually holds in any additive group, the Lean version preserves the vector-space context of the informal statement without adding or dropping anything material.

The informal claim asserts that the intersection of any collection of subspaces of a vector space V is a subspace. The Lean statement sets V as a module over a field F (i.e., a vector space), takes an arbitrary indexed family u : ι → Submodule F V (which encodes "any collection" of subspaces), and concludes ∃ U : Submodule F V, (⋂ i, (u i).carrier) = ↑U — i.e., the set-theoretic intersection is the underlying set of some submodule, which is precisely "the intersection is a subspace." Since (u i).carrier equals the coercion to Set by Submodule.mem_carrier, and the witness U = ⨅ i, u i exists by Submodule.coe_iInf, this is logically equivalent to the informal claim. No hypotheses are dropped or added, no types are changed, and the quantifier structure matches.

The Lean signature faithfully captures the informal statement. It quantifies over a field F, a vector space V over F (given by AddCommGroup plus Module F V), a linear map T : V →ₗ[F] V, assumes finrank F V = 1 (matching "dim V = 1"), and concludes ∃ c : F, ∀ v : V, T v = c • v, which matches "∃ a ∈ F such that Tv = av for all v". The extra [FiniteDimensional F V] instance is redundant because in Mathlib `finrank K V = 0` whenever V is not finite-dimensional, so `finrank F V = 1` already implies FiniteDimensional; it neither weakens nor strengthens the claim. The quantifier structure, types, and conclusion match the informal statement exactly.

The Lean statement captures exactly the informal claim. `hm : m = p.degree` (with the natural coercion `m : ℕ → WithBot ℕ`) encodes "p has degree m" as in the informal statement. `@card (p.rootSet ℂ) = m` says p has exactly m distinct roots in ℂ (since `rootSet` is a finset of roots without multiplicity). `Disjoint (p.rootSet ℂ) ((derivative p).rootSet ℂ)` says p and p' share no roots. The `hp : p ≠ 0` hypothesis is redundant (implied by `hm` since `m : ℕ` implies `p.degree ≠ ⊥`), but surfacing this implicit premise does not change the meaning or narrow the scope beyond the informal statement. The edge case m = 0 (nonzero constant) works: both sides are vacuously true since `rootSet` of a nonzero constant is ∅ and `rootSet 0 = ∅`. No drift in types, quantifier structure, hypotheses, or conclusion.

The Lean statement faithfully encodes the informal claim. V is a vector space over F (via `Field F` and `Module F V`), S and T are linear operators in L(V), and `S ∘ T = T ∘ S` (function composition via FunLike coercion) is equivalent to ST = TS as linear maps. The conclusion `Submodule.map S (ker (T - c • LinearMap.id)) ≤ ker (T - c • LinearMap.id)` precisely says S(null(T - cI)) ⊆ null(T - cI), i.e., the null space is invariant under S. The free variable c : F corresponds to the universal quantification "for every λ ∈ F". Working over an arbitrary field rather than specifically ℝ/ℂ is a natural reading since the informal statement does not restrict F and the result holds generally.

The Lean signature correctly formalizes Axler 5.12. Using Mathlib's `eigenspace S c = {x | S x = c • x}`, the hypothesis `∀ v, ∃ c, v ∈ eigenspace S c` states that every vector satisfies `S v = c • v` for some c — this is equivalent to "every (nonzero) vector is an eigenvector" since the v=0 case is trivially in every eigenspace. The conclusion `∃ c : F, S = c • LinearMap.id` is precisely "S is a scalar multiple of the identity operator". The statement uses arbitrary field F and arbitrary F-module V, without assuming finite-dimensionality — this matches the informal statement, which does not require finite-dimensionality (and the claim indeed holds without it). No hypotheses are silently added or dropped, and the quantifier structure matches exactly.

The Lean formalization faithfully captures the informal statement. (1) `card (T.Eigenvalues) = finrank F V` correctly expresses "T has dim V distinct eigenvalues" since `T.Eigenvalues` is the subtype of distinct eigenvalues in Mathlib and `finrank F V` is dim V in the finite-dimensional setting. (2) The hypothesis `∀ v : V, (∃ c : F, v ∈ eigenspace S c) ↔ (∃ c : F, v ∈ eigenspace T c)` correctly expresses "S has the same eigenvectors as T": for nonzero v this says v is eigenvector of S iff eigenvector of T, and for v = 0 both sides are trivially true (0 lies in every eigenspace), so including 0 doesn't alter the meaning. (3) `S * T = T * S` is the standard encoding of ST = TS for endomorphisms. (4) The FiniteDimensional hypothesis is an implicit premise needed for "dim V" to be well-defined — surfacing it is appropriate and faithful, not weaker.

The Lean formalization faithfully captures Axler's Exercise 6.2. The typeclass setup `[NormedAddCommGroup V] [RCLike F] [InnerProductSpace F V]` is the standard Mathlib encoding of "V is an inner product space over F" where F is ℝ or ℂ — matching Axler's convention that F ∈ {ℝ, ℂ}. The inner product notation `⟪u, v⟫_F = 0` uses the scoped notation from `InnerProductSpace` correctly resolving to `inner F u v`. The right-hand side `∀ (a : F), ‖u‖ ≤ ‖u + a • v‖` directly translates "‖u‖ ≤ ‖u+av‖ for all a ∈ F", and the scalar multiplication `a • v` is the correct action. The iff structure and quantifier order exactly match the informal statement. No hypotheses are dropped or added, and the conclusion is logically equivalent.

The Lean statement matches Mathlib's `inner_eq_sum_norm_sq_div_four` verbatim. Although the signs of the imaginary terms appear swapped compared to Axler's informal statement, this is because Mathlib's inner product is conjugate-linear in the FIRST argument (physics convention: `inner_smul_left : ⟪r•x, y⟫ = r† * ⟪x, y⟫`), whereas Axler uses the math convention (linear in the first argument). Under the relation ⟨u,v⟩_Axler = conj(⟪u,v⟫_Mathlib), taking the conjugate of Axler's identity flips the sign of the imaginary part, yielding precisely the Lean expression `(‖u+v‖² - ‖u-v‖² + (‖u - I•v‖² - ‖u + I•v‖²)·I)/4`. Thus the Lean statement expresses the same mathematical content as Axler's claim, correctly translated into Mathlib's convention. Hypotheses (complex inner product space, any u, v) match the informal statement exactly.

The informal statement "U⊥ = {0} iff U = V" is FALSE in a general inner product space without further hypotheses: (1) it fails when U is dense but not closed (e.g., U = ℓ²_fin in V = ℓ²); (2) it fails even for closed U in an incomplete inner product space (e.g., V = ℓ²_fin with U = kernel of ⟨·,v₀⟩ for v₀ ∈ ℓ²\V has U⊥ = {0} yet U ≠ V). The Lean formalization adds exactly `CompleteSpace V` and `IsClosed U`, which are the minimal hypotheses needed to make the iff hold — these are the canonical Mathlib conditions (they give `HasOrthogonalProjection`, under which `Submodule.orthogonal_eq_bot_iff` is a true theorem). Since these hypotheses are strictly necessary for the statement's truth (not extra scope-narrowing), adding them counts as surfacing implicit premises rather than weakening. The types (Submodule K V, ⊥ for {0}, ⊤ for V, RCLike K for the scalar field of an inner product space) are the standard Mathlib encodings of the informal objects.

The Lean formalization correctly captures the informal statement. The operator space `End F V` corresponds to $\mathcal{L}(V)$, normality is correctly encoded as `T * adjoint T = adjoint T * T` (where multiplication in `End F V` is composition, matching $TT^* = T^*T$), and the conclusion `range T = range (adjoint T)` directly mirrors $\operatorname{range} T = \operatorname{range} T^*$. The scalar field uses `RCLike F` for ℝ or ℂ, matching the standard inner-product-space context. The `FiniteDimensional F V` hypothesis is a necessary implicit premise: Mathlib's `LinearMap.adjoint` (which is what `adjoint T` resolves to for `T : End F V`) is only defined under `FiniteDimensional`, so without it the expression would not typecheck, and the informal use of $T^*$ presumes the adjoint exists. Surfacing this implicit premise is a faithful reading.

The Lean signature matches the informal statement: V is a complex inner product space (via NormedAddCommGroup + InnerProductSpace ℂ), T is a linear operator (T : End ℂ V ≃ V →ₗ[ℂ] V), the normality hypothesis `T * adjoint T = adjoint T * T` corresponds to T T* = T* T, and T^9 = T^8 matches. The conclusion `IsSelfAdjoint T ∧ T^2 = T` correctly encodes "T is self-adjoint and T² = T". The extra `FiniteDimensional ℂ V` hypothesis is surfacing an implicit premise required for the statement to make sense: Mathlib's `LinearMap.adjoint` is only defined under `[FiniteDimensional 𝕜 E]`, and the notion of "normal operator" with an adjoint requires this (or a Hilbert-space assumption) to be well-formed. This matches the standard mathematical convention (and Axler's usage) where adjoints are taken in finite-dimensional inner-product spaces. Hence this is a faithful surfacing of an implicit premise, not a narrowing.

The Lean statement faithfully encodes Axler 7.14. `[RCLike 𝕜]` captures 𝐅 ∈ {ℝ, ℂ}; `[InnerProductSpace 𝕜 V]` with `[FiniteDimensional 𝕜 V]` captures the implicit finite-dimensional inner product space context in Axler's chapter 7 (needed so the adjoint/self-adjoint notion is well-defined in Mathlib). `IsSelfAdjoint T` on `Module.End 𝕜 V` is exactly `T.adjoint = T` via the `Star` instance defined by adjoint in `Adjoint.lean`. The hypothesis `∃ v, ‖v‖ = 1 ∧ ‖T v - l • v‖ < ε` precisely matches the informal assumption, and `∃ l' : T.Eigenvalues, ‖l - l'‖ < ε` matches the conclusion: `T.Eigenvalues = {μ // T.HasEigenvalue μ}` coerces to `𝕜` via `CoeOut`, and the norm on an `RCLike` field equals `|·|`. The implication direction, quantifiers, types, and norms all match the informal statement.

The informal statement asks to prove addition in ℤ/nℤ is associative. The Lean version states `(x + y) + z = x + (y + z)` for all `x y z : ZMod n` and all `n : ℕ`. In Mathlib, `ZMod n` is the canonical implementation of ℤ/nℤ (defined as `ℤ` when n = 0, `Fin n` otherwise), with addition corresponding exactly to residue-class addition. The universal quantification over n : ℕ matches the natural reading of the informal claim, which speaks of ℤ/nℤ generally. No hypotheses are added or dropped, and the conclusion matches the associativity law precisely.

The informal statement asserts that for n > 1, (ℤ/nℤ, ·) is not a group. Since ZMod n is already a commutative monoid with identity 1 (it's a CommRing in Mathlib), and the identity of a monoid is unique, the question of whether the multiplicative structure is a group reduces exactly to whether every element has a two-sided multiplicative inverse relative to 1. The Lean statement `¬ (∀ x : ZMod n, ∃ y : ZMod n, x * y = 1 ∧ y * x = 1)` negates precisely this condition. The hypothesis `1 < n` matches "n > 1" in the informal statement, and the outer `n : ℕ` with hypothesis acts as the universal quantifier. This is a direct and logically equivalent encoding of "ZMod n is not a group under multiplication".

The Lean statement quantifies over arbitrary groups G and arbitrary elements x, matching the informal statement's implicit universal quantification. The notation |x| in standard group theory (Dummit-Foote) denotes the order of x, which is faithfully encoded as `orderOf x` in Mathlib. Checking the iff in both directions: if x^2 = 1 then orderOf x ∣ 2 so orderOf x ∈ {1, 2}; conversely orderOf x = 1 implies x = 1 (via `orderOf_eq_one_iff`) hence x^2 = 1, and orderOf x = 2 directly gives x^2 = 1 via `pow_orderOf_eq_one`. The statement also handles Mathlib's convention (orderOf = 0 for infinite order) correctly: infinite-order x has x^2 ≠ 1 and neither disjunct holds. No hypotheses are added, dropped, or altered.

The informal statement is a chain of equivalences "A iff B iff C" with A: xy=yx, B: y⁻¹xy=x, C: x⁻¹y⁻¹xy=1. The Lean encoding (A ↔ B) ∧ (B ↔ C) is the standard formalization of such a chain — it gives mutual equivalence of all three propositions (A ↔ C follows by transitivity). The group G is arbitrary with [Group G], matching the informal "elements of G". Parenthesization of y⁻¹*x*y and x⁻¹*y⁻¹*x*y correctly represents y⁻¹xy and x⁻¹y⁻¹xy via left-associative multiplication. No hypotheses are added or dropped, and the types match exactly.

The Lean statement is a direct and logically equivalent translation of the informal claim. Mathlib's `orderOf x` represents |x| (the order of a group element, with 0 encoding infinite order), matching the textbook notation. The informal statement's implicit universal quantification over arbitrary elements x, g of an arbitrary group G is faithfully captured by `{G : Type*} [Group G] (x g : G)`. The conclusion `orderOf x = orderOf (g⁻¹ * x * g)` precisely expresses |x| = |g⁻¹ x g|, and works correctly for both finite and infinite order (since conjugation preserves the order in both cases, and 0 = 0 holds in the infinite case).

The Lean signature captures the informal statement exactly. The type `G` with `[Group G]` represents an arbitrary group (matching the implicit context "$G$ is a group"). The hypothesis `∀ x : G, x ^ 2 = 1` is a direct transcription of "$x^2 = 1$ for all $x \in G$". The conclusion `∀ a b : G, a*b = b*a` is the standard definition of being abelian/commutative — equivalent to asserting `CommGroup G` given that `Group G` is already provided. No hypotheses are added or dropped, the quantifier structure matches, and the types are correct.

The informal statement says: if x has infinite order in G, then {x^n : n ∈ ℤ} are all distinct. The Lean formalization encodes "infinite order" as `orderOf x = 0`, which by Mathlib's `orderOf_eq_zero_iff` is definitionally equivalent to `¬IsOfFinOrder x` (the standard Mathlib rendering of "infinite order"). The claim that the powers are all distinct is correctly formulated as: for all n, m : ℤ with n ≠ m, x^n ≠ x^m — this is exactly the meaning of "all distinct" for the indexed family. G is an arbitrary group, matching the informal universal reading. The quantifier structure, types (ℤ for exponents), and conclusion all match. No hidden extra hypotheses or weakenings.

The Lean signature uses `ℝˣ` (= `Units ℝ`, the multiplicative group of nonzero reals) and `ℂˣ` (= `Units ℂ`, the multiplicative group of nonzero complexes), which are exactly the objects referenced by ℝ−{0} and ℂ−{0} viewed as multiplicative groups. The type `ℝˣ ≃* ℂˣ` is the type of multiplicative group isomorphisms between them, and `IsEmpty` states that this type has no inhabitants, i.e., no such isomorphism exists. This is precisely the informal claim that these two multiplicative groups are not isomorphic, with no added or dropped hypotheses.

The Lean statement captures exactly the informal claim. "Let G be any group" is faithfully rendered via `{G : Type*} [Group G]`. The map g ↦ g⁻¹ is precisely pinned down by the hypothesis `hf : f = λ g => g⁻¹`. The homomorphism condition `∀ x y, f x * f y = f (x*y)` is the standard multiplicative-homomorphism equation (symmetric to Mathlib's `f (x*y) = f x * f y`); for groups this is equivalent to being a monoid/group homomorphism since preservation of 1 follows automatically. "G is abelian" is correctly encoded as `∀ x y, x*y = y*x`. The overall biconditional structure matches the informal "if and only if". No hypotheses are added or removed, and the types and quantifier structure align.

The Lean signature captures exactly the informal claim. `G` is a finite group via `[Group G] [Fintype G]`, the hypothesis `card G > 2` matches "n = |G| > 2", and the conclusion quantifies universally over subgroups `H` asserting `card H ≠ card G - 1`, which is the standard encoding of "G cannot have a subgroup H with |H| = n-1". The `[Fintype H]` instance is only bookkeeping to allow `card H` to be computed (and is automatically derivable from `[Fintype G]`). Natural number subtraction `card G - 1` is well-behaved here because `card G ≥ 3`. No hypotheses are added or dropped, and the conclusion is logically equivalent to the informal assertion.

The Lean statement directly encodes "H is generated by H \ {1}" as `Subgroup.closure ((H : Set G) \ {1}) = H`. The coercion `(H : Set G)` gives the underlying set of the subgroup, `\ {1}` is set difference removing the identity, and `Subgroup.closure` is the subgroup generated by a set (defined in Mathlib/Algebra/Group/Subgroup/Lattice.lean as `sInf { K | k ⊆ K }`). The equality with H captures the "is generated by" claim. No hypotheses are added or dropped, no types are altered, and the edge case H = ⊥ also works (H \ {1} = ∅, closure ∅ = ⊥ = H). The formalization is logically equivalent to the informal statement.

The Lean statement faithfully formalizes the informal claim. The rotation subgroup is correctly encoded as `Subgroup.closure {DihedralGroup.r 1}`: since `r 1` has order `n` in `DihedralGroup n` (with `n ≠ 0`), its generated subgroup is precisely `{r k | k : ZMod n}`, the subgroup of all rotations. Maximality is correctly expressed as `R ≠ ⊤ ∧ ∀ K, R ≤ K → K = R ∨ K = ⊤`, which is logically equivalent to `IsCoatom R`, the standard definition of a maximal proper subgroup. The hypothesis `n ≠ 0` is a natural reading of "dihedral group" — in standard mathematical convention (and in Mathlib, where `DihedralGroup 0` is the infinite dihedral group), "dihedral group" without qualification usually refers to finite dihedral groups, so surfacing this is faithful, not weakening. The use of `{R} (hR : R = ...)` to name the subgroup is a logically equivalent presentation.

The informal statement asks to prove that A/B is abelian, given A is an abelian group and B is a subgroup. The Lean statement takes `[CommGroup A]` (abelian group), `B : Subgroup A` (subgroup), and concludes `∀ a b : A ⧸ B, a*b = b*a`. In Mathlib, `Subgroup.normal_of_comm` is an instance making every subgroup of a `CommGroup` automatically normal, so `A ⧸ B` inherits a group structure via `Quotient.group`, and `*` is the induced quotient multiplication. The universally quantified commutativity `∀ a b, a*b = b*a` on the quotient group is precisely the definition of A/B being abelian. Hypotheses, quantifier structure, and conclusion match exactly.

The Lean signature captures each clause of the informal statement: `I : Type*` encodes "arbitrary collection" without any countability restriction (matching the parenthetical), `[Nonempty I]` captures "nonempty collection", `H : I → Subgroup G` with `∀ i, Normal (H i)` encodes a collection of normal subgroups, and `Normal (⨅ (i : I), H i)` is the claim that the intersection is a normal subgroup. The `iInf` of subgroups (via `CompleteLattice (Subgroup G)`) equals the set-theoretic intersection on carriers, matching Mathlib's own `normal_iInf_normal` formulation. No hypotheses are silently added or dropped, and the types and quantifiers match the informal meaning.

The Lean statement `H.index = K.index * H.relindex K` under `H ≤ K` precisely encodes $[G:H] = [G:K] \cdot [K:H]$. In Mathlib, `Subgroup.index H = Nat.card (G ⧸ H)` and `H.relIndex K = (H.subgroupOf K).index`, which equals $[K:H]$ when $H \le K$; the file-local `relindex` is just an alias for `relIndex`. No finiteness hypothesis is assumed on `G`, matching the "do not assume $G$ is finite" clause — Mathlib's `Nat.card`-based `index` returns 0 for infinite quotients, and the equation still holds in that case (this is exactly Mathlib's `relIndex_mul_index` lemma). Hypothesis structure, conclusion, and generality all match the informal claim.

In Mathlib, `AddSubgroup.index` is defined as `Nat.card (G ⧸ H)`, which returns `0` when the quotient is infinite (confirmed by the lemma `index_eq_zero_iff_infinite : H.index = 0 ↔ Infinite (G ⧸ H)`). Thus `H.index = 0` precisely encodes "H has infinite index". The Lean statement asserts that every additive subgroup H ≠ ⊤ of ℚ satisfies H.index = 0, i.e., every proper subgroup has infinite index. This is logically equivalent to the informal claim "ℚ has no proper subgroups of finite index" (contrapositive: a subgroup of finite index must equal ⊤). The hypothesis `H ≠ ⊤` is the standard Mathlib encoding of "proper subgroup" and correctly excludes only the case H = ℚ where index would be 1.

The hypotheses `[CommGroup G] [IsSimpleGroup G]` faithfully represent "G is an abelian simple group". The conclusion `IsCyclic G ∧ ∃ G_fin : Fintype G, Nat.Prime (@card G G_fin)` is logically equivalent to "G ≅ Z_p for some prime p": forward, any Z/pZ is cyclic and of prime order; backward, any group of prime order is cyclic and isomorphic to Z/pZ. The `IsCyclic G` conjunct is technically redundant (implied by prime cardinality) but doesn't change the logical content. The "do not assume G is finite" caveat from the informal statement is correctly respected — no `[Fintype G]` appears among the hypotheses; finiteness is derived as part of the conclusion via `∃ G_fin : Fintype G`. Since `Fintype` is a subsingleton in Mathlib, the existential correctly captures "G is finite with prime cardinality". Thus the formalization is neither weaker nor stronger than the informal claim.

The informal claim is "subgroups of a solvable group are solvable". The Lean signature quantifies over an arbitrary group `G` with `[IsSolvable G]` and an arbitrary subgroup `H : Subgroup G`, and concludes `IsSolvable H` (where `H` is used as its coerced group type). This is the standard Mathlib encoding — indeed Mathlib's `subgroup_solvable_of_solvable` in `Mathlib/GroupTheory/Solvable.lean` has this exact signature. No extra hypotheses (e.g. finiteness, normality) are imposed and none are dropped, so the Lean statement is logically equivalent to the informal one.

The Lean statement precisely matches the informal claim. The premises ([Group G], [IsSolvable G], {H : Subgroup G}, hH : H ≠ ⊥, [H.Normal]) encode "H is a nontrivial normal subgroup of the solvable group G." The conclusion `∃ A ≤ H, A ≠ ⊥ ∧ A.Normal ∧ ∀ a b : A, a*b = b*a` states there exists a nontrivial subgroup A of G contained in H that is normal in G (since A has type `Subgroup G`, so `A.Normal` means normal in G, matching A ⊴ G) and abelian (elements commute). The bounded existential `∃ A ≤ H, ...` unfolds to `∃ A, A ≤ H ∧ ...`, so A is naturally a subgroup of G that sits inside H. The abelianness encoding `∀ a b : A, a*b = b*a` is logically equivalent to A being abelian. No hypotheses are added or dropped, no types are altered, and the quantifier structure matches.

The Lean signature faithfully encodes the informal statement. "Composite order" is captured by `¬ (card G).Prime ∧ card G ≠ 1`: since `[Fintype G] [Group G]` implies `card G ≥ 1`, this precisely characterizes the composite numbers {4, 6, 8, 9, ...}. The "positive integer k dividing n" condition is captured by `∀ k : ℕ, k ∣ card G → ...`, which is equivalent to the informal version because in ℕ, `0 ∣ n ↔ n = 0`, and `card G ≠ 0`; thus k = 0 is automatically excluded. The subgroup existence `∃ (H : Subgroup G) (fH : Fintype H), @card H fH = k` correctly expresses "G has a subgroup of order k". The conclusion `¬ IsSimpleGroup G` matches "G is not simple". Quantifier structure, types, hypotheses, and conclusion all align.

The Lean signature matches the informal claim exactly. The ambient set A is encoded as `α` with `[Fintype α]` and `Fintype.card α > 1` captures |A|>1. The permutation group G is `Subgroup (Equiv.Perm α)`. Transitivity is encoded as `∀ a b, ∃ σ : G, (σ : Equiv.Perm α) a = b`, which is precisely the Mathlib definition `MulAction.IsPretransitive.exists_smul_eq`; since |A|>1 implies α nonempty, this coincides with classical transitivity. The conclusion `∃ σ : G, ∀ a : α, (σ : Equiv.Perm α) a ≠ a` is a literal rendering of "some σ ∈ G with σ(a) ≠ a for all a ∈ A". No hypothesis is added or dropped, and the quantifier structure is preserved.

The informal statement asserts that every characteristic subgroup of any group is normal. The Lean signature quantifies over an arbitrary `Group G` and an arbitrary `Subgroup G`, assumes `[Characteristic H]` (Mathlib's `Subgroup.Characteristic` class, which encodes the standard definition: fixed under all automorphisms of G), and concludes `Normal H`. This is exactly the informal claim — no extra hypotheses, no weakened conclusion, no change in quantifier structure or ambient objects. In fact, Mathlib itself proves this as the instance `normal_of_characteristic` in the same file. The formalization is strictly faithful.

The Lean statement faithfully captures the informal claim. The hypothesis `∀ K, ∀ fK : Fintype K, card H = card K → H = K` correctly encodes "H is the unique subgroup of its order": any finite subgroup K with the same cardinality as H must equal H (any K of infinite cardinality trivially cannot equal the finite H). The `[Fintype H]` assumption surfaces the implicit premise that "a given order" refers to a finite cardinality, which is the standard mathematical reading of "order" in this context. The conclusion `H.Characteristic` matches Mathlib's definition (H fixed by all automorphisms), which is exactly the textbook meaning of characteristic subgroup. The finiteness assumption is needed to formalize the notion of "order" as a natural number and to apply the uniqueness hypothesis to φ(H) for automorphisms φ.

The Lean statement unfolds exactly the definition of "P.subgroupOf H is a Sylow p-subgroup of H". In Mathlib, Sylow p G is a structure with two fields: isPGroup' (IsPGroup p toSubgroup) and is_maximal' (maximality among p-subgroups). The Lean conclusion is a conjunction of these two properties applied to P.subgroupOf H inside H, which is the standard encoding of "P ∈ Syl_p(H)" when P ≤ H. The hypothesis hH : P ≤ H captures "H contains P" (the coercion from Sylow to Subgroup makes this well-typed). No extra hypotheses are introduced and the quantifier structure matches the Mathlib Sylow definition exactly.

The Lean statement assumes G is a finite group with `card G = 312` and claims there exists a prime p, with p dividing card G, and a Sylow p-subgroup P of G that is normal. This directly matches the informal statement "a group of order 312 has a normal Sylow p-subgroup for some prime p dividing its order." The Mathlib `Sylow p G` structure represents maximal p-subgroups, and combined with the `p.Prime` hypothesis captures the standard notion of a Sylow p-subgroup. The `P.Normal` predicate corresponds to normality of the underlying subgroup, and the quantifier structure (∃ p, ∃ P, ...) faithfully matches "for some prime p ... normal Sylow p-subgroup".

The Lean statement captures exactly the informal claim. The hypotheses `hpqr : p < q ∧ q < r`, `hpqr1 : p.Prime ∧ q.Prime ∧ r.Prime`, and `hG : card G = p*q*r` faithfully encode "|G| = pqr with primes p < q < r". The conclusion is a three-way disjunction of existence of a normal Sylow p-, q-, or r-subgroup, which matches "G has a normal Sylow subgroup for either p, q or r". `[Fintype G]` is required for `card G` to make sense and is not an added restriction since pqr ≥ 2 forces finiteness. `P.Normal` for `P : Sylow p G` resolves via the `Sylow extends Subgroup G` structure to `Subgroup.Normal`, the standard notion. No hypothesis is dropped or added beyond what is logically implicit.

The Lean statement correctly formalizes the informal claim. `[Fintype G] [Group G]` with `hG : card G = 200` (where `card` resolves to `Fintype.card` via `open Fintype`) exactly expresses 'a group of order 200'. The conclusion `∃ N : Sylow 5 G, N.Normal` says there exists a Sylow 5-subgroup that is normal — which is precisely 'has a normal Sylow 5-subgroup'. The `Sylow 5 G` structure from Mathlib's GroupTheory/Sylow.lean encodes maximal 5-subgroups, and `N.Normal` is the standard normality predicate inherited via the coercion to `Subgroup G`. The implicit universal quantification over G is captured by the theorem's type variables. No hypotheses are added or dropped, and the quantifier structure matches.

The informal statement "if |G|=1365 then G is not simple" is universally quantified over groups G. The Lean signature quantifies over a finite group G (via `Fintype G` and `Group G`), requires `card G = 1365` (resolving to `Fintype.card` via `open Fintype`), and concludes `¬ IsSimpleGroup G`. Mathlib's `IsSimpleGroup` is defined as `Nontrivial G` together with the condition that every normal subgroup equals ⊥ or ⊤, which is the standard textbook definition. The `Fintype` instance is implicit in the informal statement (having order 1365 implies finiteness). No hypotheses are added or removed relative to the informal reading, and the quantifier structure and conclusion match exactly.

The informal statement says: if |G| = 132 then G is not simple. The Lean statement formalizes this by taking a group G with `[Fintype G]` and `card G = 132` (i.e., Fintype.card G = 132, since `open Fintype` is used), then asserting `¬ IsSimpleGroup G`.
